![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2315339/38869206-52ffa17c-4263-11e8-8d51-e8e782b40483.png)

It seems that TabSeparatedWithNamesAndTypes returns format description with timezone, which is not parsed well in (It is handled as simple field and add Field after brackets):

```Python
    @classmethod
    def create_ad_hoc_field(cls, db_type):
        import infi.clickhouse_orm.fields as orm_fields
        # Enums
        if db_type.startswith('Enum'):
            return orm_fields.BaseEnumField.create_ad_hoc_field(db_type)
        # Arrays
        if db_type.startswith('Array'):
            inner_field = cls.create_ad_hoc_field(db_type[6 : -1])
            return orm_fields.ArrayField(inner_field)
        # FixedString
        if db_type.startswith('FixedString'):
            length = int(db_type[12 : -1])
            return orm_fields.FixedStringField(length)
        # Nullable
        if db_type.startswith('Nullable'):
            inner_field = cls.create_ad_hoc_field(db_type[9 : -1])
            return orm_fields.NullableField(inner_field)
        # Simple fields
        name = db_type + 'Field'
        if not hasattr(orm_fields, name):
            raise NotImplementedError('No field class for %s' % db_type)
        return getattr(orm_fields, name)()
```

issue_title: GeoSeries.touches(other) and GeoSeries.intersects(other) unexpected behavior
issue_body:
So using these two datasets, we ran into some unexpected behavior of the intersect and touches functions.
```python
assigned_bins = cc.read('assigned_hexbins')
unassigned_bins = cc.read('unassigned_hexbins')
```

[assigned_hexbins.csv.zip](https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/files/2091578/assigned_hexbins.csv.zip)
[unassigned_hexbins.csv.zip](https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/files/2091579/unassigned_hexbins.csv.zip)

We converted the dataframes into geodataframes 
```python
unassigned_bins =cc.query('SELECT *, ST_AsText(the_geom) as geom FROM mspichigercarto.unassigned_hexbins').reset_index()
assigned_bins =cc.query('SELECT *, ST_AsText(the_geom) as geom FROM mspichigercarto.assigned_hexbins').reset_index()

import shapely.wkt

geometry = unassigned_bins['geom'].map(shapely.wkt.loads)
unassigned_bins = unassigned_bins.drop('geom', axis=1)
crs = {'init': 'epsg:4326'}
unassigned_bins_geo = gpd.GeoDataFrame(unassigned_bins, crs=crs, geometry=geometry)`
import shapely.wkt

geometry = assigned_bins['geom'].map(shapely.wkt.loads)
assigned_bins = assigned_bins.drop('geom', axis=1)
crs = {'init': 'epsg:4326'}
assigned_bins_geo = gpd.GeoDataFrame(assigned_bins, crs=crs, geometry=geometry)
```

Then we tried running touches and intersects on them, but instead of returning expected values it would return false for everything and none of the geometries intersected. 

`assigned_bins_geo.touches(unassigned_bins_geo)`

We then ran this query in cartoframes

```python
st_touch_query = '''
SELECT assigned_hexbins.*,
      unassigned_hexbins.cartodb_id as index_unassigned
FROM assigned_hexbins, unassigned_hexbins
WHERE ST_Touches(assigned_hexbins.the_geom, unassigned_hexbins.the_geom)
'''
```
according to this query, the hexbins are touching so touches and intersects are not working as expected.

issue_title: Getting S4cmd working for Wasabi or Other object storage services
issue_body:
I noticed that the s4cmd was not working with other object store except amazon. The reason being that the endpoint url was not being passed when creating a boto client . So i made the change to the python file to grab the config when specified in the s3cmd config file and use it . 
Just wanted to place it here to see if any contributor wants to add this to the tool if not somebody can use it if needed.

So the basic diff is as followsindex 3c0d683..f39b5a5 100755
```


--- a/s4cmd.py
+++ b/s4cmd.py
@@ -61,7 +61,6 @@ S3_ACCESS_KEY_NAME = "S3_ACCESS_KEY"
 S3_SECRET_KEY_NAME = "S3_SECRET_KEY"
 S4CMD_ENV_KEY = "S4CMD_OPTS"

@@ -377,12 +375,16 @@ class BotoClient(object):
        "If the bucket is configured as a website, redirects requests for this object to another object in the same bucket or to an external URL. Amazon S3 stores the value of this header in the object metadata."),
   ]
 
-  def __init__(self, opt, aws_access_key_id=None, aws_secret_access_key=None):
+  def __init__(self, opt, aws_access_key_id=None, aws_secret_access_key=None, aws_access_endpoint=None):
     '''Initialize boto3 API bridge class. Calculate and cache all legal parameters
        for each method we are going to call.
     '''
     self.opt = opt
-    if (aws_access_key_id is not None) and (aws_secret_access_key is not None):
+    if(aws_access_key_id is not None) and (aws_secret_access_key is not None) and (aws_access_endpoint is not None):
+       self.client = self.boto3.client('s3',
+                                      aws_access_key_id=aws_access_key_id,
+                                      aws_secret_access_key=aws_secret_access_key,endpoint_url=aws_access_endpoint)
+    elif (aws_access_key_id is not None) and (aws_secret_access_key is not None):
       self.client = self.boto3.client('s3',
                                       aws_access_key_id=aws_access_key_id,
                                       aws_secret_access_key=aws_secret_access_key)
@@ -624,6 +626,7 @@ class S3Handler(object):
   '''
 
   S3_KEYS = None
+  S3_ENDPOINT = None
 
   @staticmethod
   def s3_keys_from_env():
@@ -664,12 +667,33 @@ class S3Handler(object):
     except Exception as e:
       info("could not read S3 keys from %s file; skipping (%s)", s3cfg_path, e)
       return None
+  
+  @staticmethod
+  def s3_endpoint_from_s3cfg(opt):
+    '''Retrieve S3 endpoint settings from s3cmd's config file, if present; otherwise return None.'''
+    try:
+      if opt.s3cfg != None:

+        return None
+      config = ConfigParser.ConfigParser()
+      config.read(s3cfg_path)
+      endpoint = "https://" + config.get("default", "host_base")
+      debug("read S3 endpoint from $HOME/.s3cfg file")
+      debug(endpoint)
+      return endpoint
+    except Exception as e:
+      info("could not read S3 keys from %s file; skipping (%s)", s3cfg_path, e)
+      return None
 
   @staticmethod
   def init_s3_keys(opt):
     '''Initialize s3 access keys from environment variable or s3cfg config file.'''
     S3Handler.S3_KEYS = S3Handler.s3_keys_from_cmdline(opt) or S3Handler.s3_keys_from_env() \
                         or S3Handler.s3_keys_from_s3cfg(opt)
+    S3Handler.S3_ENDPOINT = S3Handler.s3_endpoint_from_s3cfg(opt)
 
   def __init__(self, opt):

@@ -684,7 +708,9 @@ class S3Handler(object):

         self.s3 = BotoClient(self.opt, S3Handler.S3_KEYS[0], S3Handler.S3_KEYS[1])
       else:

issue_title: Can not reopen Pub/Sub subscription with a different callback
issue_body:
I'm trying to do a simple thing: open Pub/Sub subscription with one callback just to drain out all messages that are currently stored in the subscription. Then reopen the same subscription with a different callback to do a proper processing. Unfortunately that doesn't seem to work.

The first time I open subscription like this:
```python
    drained_num = [0]

    def discard_callback(message):
        drained_num[0] += 1
        message.ack()

    future = subscription.open(discard_callback)

    try:
        future.result(timeout=2)
    except TimeoutError:
        pass
    except Exception as ex:
        logger.error("Caught unexpected exception!", exc_info=True)
    finally:
        logger.info("Discarded %d messages", drained_num[0])
        subscription.close()
```

After that I try to reopen subscription with a different callback:
```python
    def callback(message):
        pass

    future = subscription.open(callback)

    try:
        future.result(timeout=3)
    except TimeoutError:
        logger.info("Time is up!")
    except Exception as ex:
        logger.error("Caught unexpected exception!", exc_info=True)
    finally:
        logger.info("Closing connection")
        subscription.close()
```

And this fails with the following error:
```
MainThread:139861846668480 [ERROR] Caught unexpected exception!
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "scripts/publish-receive.py", line 85, in main
    future.result(timeout=args.timeout)
  File "PATH/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/futures.py", line 103, in result
    raise err
RuntimeError: cannot schedule new futures after shutdown
```

I tried to don't call `subscription.close()` after reading subscription the first time but then I get another error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "scripts/publish-receive.py", line 98, in <module>
    main()
  File "scripts/publish-receive.py", line 81, in main
    future = subscription.open(callback)
  File "PATH/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/policy/thread.py", line 261, in open
    raise ValueError('This policy has already been opened.')
ValueError: This policy has already been opened.
```

So, my question is it even possible to reopen subscription with a different callback?
I'm using `google-cloud-pubsub-0.30.1`.

issue_title: Add a Task 'implementation version', to allow Task implementers to invalidate artifacts
issue_body:
The implementation version would be a (string) property of a Task that a Task implementer could use to invalidate all artifacts for the Task.

Additionally, in the case of `Task.incremental==True`, the implementation version should be used to skip incrementalism. The easiest way to do this would likely be to include the current implementation version in the `results_dir` path, and to _not_ persist it in the build invalidator.

issue_title: Drop support for python 3.2
issue_body:
Quoted from the original description in #263

> From #255

> The new versions of pylint and astroid have dropped support for Python 2.6 (and presumably also for python 3.2). At the moment, we have pinned to old versions in requirements-dev.txt. We will have to decide what to do about our own Python 2.6 support going forward. One option may be to have Travis use different versions of pylon etc for Py2.6, but this seems a bit messy.

I suggest to split the discussion up, and in this issue only discuss dropping 3.2

This will not solve the problem with dropped support for old versions in some of the projects we depend on, but will get us rid of some of the old version in the support. At least python 3.2 have some annoying coding requirements that it would be nice to get rid of.

One problem is that I think the current version of Raspbian (Debian for Raspberry Pis) still replies on Pythons 3.2 and since people are doing fun things with Raspberry Pis and SoCo, I don't think we should pull support until some time after Raspbian has upgraded. I will see if I can find some information about their support plan.

Python 3.2 is from February 11.

issue_title: django-pyodbc and DBISAM ODBC driver
issue_body:
I am working to get a Django project to talk with a DBISAM 4.21 database using the pyodbc and django-pyodbc software and can find little to nothing about how to make this work. It seems to me that DBISAM is a relatively obscure proprietary database server software based on SQL-92 standards. It is not listed as supported by pyodbc at this time but after opening an issue with them in their Git repo I'm able to initiate a connection with a DSN from within the Python shell:

`pyodbc.connect(r'DSN=databasename;UID=User;PWD=Password’)`

The company that makes the software (Elevate Software) claims on their website that they support connections from non Delphi based projects using their provided ODBC driver (http://www.elevatesoft.com/products?action=order&category=dbisam&type=other).

I'm trying to use the DBISAM 4.21 database because I am working on interfacing an existing software written in Delphi that stores it's data in the DBISAM database with a new Python 3 + Django 1.10 based software that is yet to be written.

We have a test environment with Windows 2012 R2 running the DBISAM server software. It has the ODBC driver from the vendor installed along with the vendors Delphi based application. We have also loaded Python 3.6 along with Django 1.10.5 so we can try and get a connection working.

I am completely new to the world of DBISAM database technology AND completely new to the Python ODBC world so let me know if I can offer more details on anything to help the process.

When I load the following configuration into my settings.py file in Django as such:

`
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        # 'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
        # 'NAME': os.path.join(BASE_DIR, 'db.sqlite3'),
        'ENGINE': 'django_pyodbc',

        'USER': 'username',
        'PASSWORD': 'password',

}
`

I get the following output

`pyodbc.Error: ('08001', '[08001] [Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][DBNETLIB]SQL Server does not exist or access denied. (17) (SQLDriverConnect)')`

I am wondering if anyone could offer any thoughts to help me get this connected?

issue_title: Results from last soap request are stored indefinitely on the service object
issue_body:
Hi, I'm not sure if this was intended but once a service object has been built and a request issued, the results are stored in memory through the service object even if the application `del`s the returned data structure:

```python
import suds.cache
from googleads.adwords import AdWordsClient
from googleads.oauth2 import GoogleRefreshTokenClient

client = AdWordsClient(
    developer_token,
    GoogleRefreshTokenClient(
        client_id,
        client_secret,
        refresh_token,
    ),
    user_agent,
    cache=suds.cache.ObjectCache(
        location=suds_cache_path,
        **suds_cache_duration
    )
)

client.SetClientCustomerId(customer_id)

service = client.GetService(service_name, version=service_version, server=server)
service.get(predicates) # note that the result is dropped on the floor here

for child in service._method_proxies['get'].__closure__[1].cell_contents.method.binding['input'].multiref.nodes[0].children[0].children:
    print(child) # prints out the results of the previous service.get call
```

I'm finding this to be a problem because in my application I frequently keep the service objects around to avoid the overhead of loading the WSDL. The memory from 25 cached soap services, all holding their latest result set, can eat up a lot of resources on my servers. I'm running `googleads==6.1.0` in production, but I'm able to reproduce the same behavior on `googleads==8.1.0`.

Here's a snippet of my objgraph output, if it helps any:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6719894/32250531-478fa46a-be63-11e7-9dd0-ac770d4189d8.png)

Thanks!

issue_title: Array of array problem in validation
issue_body:
I have problem with system which want on input dict, which is located in two arrays. I have this variable in Python, which I passing as argument to soap function:

variables_key = [
    [
        {
            'IsKey': True,
            'Key': 'GUID',
            'Value': '2905a75f-414d-44d5-ba0c-c4307c92666f'
         }
    ]
]

In this attachment is WSDL regarding types used in this application. Argument is using type ArrayOfArrayOfKeyValuePair.
[wsdl.xml.txt](https://github.com/pysimplesoap/pysimplesoap/files/32039/wsdl.xml.txt)


When I run query with this argument, script fails with error TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict' in client.py:465. I've tried some debugging and I believe that there is some bug in validation function or in completing struct. Issue is that struct seems like that:
[{u'ArrayOfKeyValuePair': [{u'KeyValuePair': {u'IsKey': <type 'bool'>, u'Key': <type 'str'>, u'Value': <type 'str'>}}]}]

When validation function starts validating first array, it consider struct as dict (because struct is u'ArrayOfKeyValuePair': ...) and starts iterating list. So it takes first value of list (which is dict) and test if it's in struct. And condition dict in dict fails because dict can't be key of dict.

issue_title: Always having an AllWatcher can be expensive on large models
issue_body: @rogpeppe brought up that having an AllWatcher running can get expensive on large models, especially if all you want to do is deploy and / or query the state of a single application, for instance.  Perhaps we can look into lazy-starting the watcher, having an option to use more fine-grained watchers (may require small changes to core), or some other approach.  At least it's worth doing some benchmarking against large models.

issue_title: String representation of extended communities occasionally empty
issue_body:
<!---
Hello,

We are sorry that you are experiencing an issue with ExaBGP.

Before opening this issue could you please:
 - make sure the problem was not already reported to avoid duplicates.
 - check if the problem is still present on the latest version
   using the 3.4 or master branch (if you know what it means)
   alternatively the latest release (3.4 or 4.0)

For us to help you the outpout of exabgp -d is often required.
Please use ``` for any copied output.
-->

##### Summary
<!---
Explain the problem briefly
-->

When dumping messages in JSON format, the string representation of extended communities is occasionally empty.

```
"extended-community": [ { "value": 615035021822126, "string": "target:12127:2222" }, { "value": 217861631974049025, "string": "" } ]
```

##### OS
<!---
The exact OS would be appreciated (vendor and version)
(as OSes affect the way Python behave on some corner cases)
-->
```
Debian 3.16.56-1+deb8u1 (2018-05-08) x86_64
```

<!--
the output of `uname -a`
-->
```
Linux <XXX> 3.16.0-6-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 3.16.56-1+deb8u1 (2018-05-08) x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

##### Version
<!--
Paste verbatim the output from “exabgp --version” between quotes below
-->
```
ExaBGP : 4.0.6-daa3d6ba
Python : 2.7.9 (default, Jun 29 2016, 13:08:31)  [GCC 4.9.2]
Uname  : Linux <XXX> 3.16.0-6-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 3.16.56-1+deb8u1 (2018-05-08) x86_64
Root   : /usr/local
```

##### Installation
<!--
Please let us know how you installed ExaBGP 
-->
```
git clone && python setup.py (if I remember correctly).
```

##### Environment 
<!--
Paste verbatim the output from “exabgp --di” (let us know if the output is empty) between quotes below
You can also use gist.github.com links for larger files 
-->

```
[exabgp.api]
encoder = text
respawn = false

[exabgp.daemon]
user = 'exabgp'
```

##### Configuration
<!--- 
Paste verbatim your configuration file
-->
```
process log-routes {
  run bash -c 'cat >> /home/<XXX>/bgp.log';
  encoder json;
}
neighbor <XXX> {
  router-id <XXX>;
  local-address <XXX>;
  local-as <XXX>; # IGP, local and peer AS are the same
  peer-as <XXX>;
  hold-time 240;
  adj-rib-in false;
  capability {
    add-path receive;
  }
  api {
    processes [log-routes];
    neighbor-changes;
    receive {
      parsed;
      update;
    }
  }
}

```

##### Program output
<!-- 
When encountering a problem please run exabgp with the "-d" option

THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT INFORMATION - WITHOUT IT WE ARE UNLIKELY TO BE ABLE TO HELP
-->
I have a huge dump from which I extracted the debug and json information. The contained data is customer sensitive, so I would like to share the data privately. Feel free to provide a mail address in the comments where I can send the relevant information.

##### Steps to reproduce
<!--
If the issue is intermittent please provide how it can be reproduced
-->
I haven't found a way to reproduce the bug up to now. It is probably related to some multithreading or timing issue. I guess, this is triggered by the if statement in lib/exabgp/bgp/message/update/attribute/community/extended/community.py, line 93. However, my knowledge of the sources is not deep enough to further debug this.

```
        def json (self):
                h = 0x00
                for byte in self.community:
                        h <<= 8
                        h += ordinal(byte)
                s = self.klass.__repr__(self) if self.klass else ''
                return '{ "value": %s, "string": "%s" }' % (h,s)
```

##### Importance
<!--
Please let us know if the issue is affecting you in a production environment
-->
This bug is affecting our productive environment because we are relying on the decoded string representation for further processing. We could decode the  community from the value field ourselves, but would prefer a proper solution in the ExaBGP code base.

issue_title: Client.create_service(networks=...) fails
issue_body:
With `docker.py==1.10.2` trying to create service with networks settings fails with:
```
500 Server Error: Internal Server Error ("{"message":"json: cannot unmarshal string into Go value of type swarm.NetworkAttachmentConfig"}")
```
There is no documentation example how to handle it properly so i look for example in tests... Nothing.

Docker go sources defines [NetworkAttachmentConfig](https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/3ea762b9f6ba256cf51bd2c35988f0c48bccf0b0/api/types/swarm/network.go#L72) like dict of two keys.

Docker-py documentation states for `networks (list): List of network names or IDs to attach the service to. Default: None.` which is false and **untested**.

Temporary workaroud: 
```
Client.create_serivce(networks=[{'Target': 'some_network_name'}])
```

issue_title: Execute function can't handle redirects
issue_body:
I'm a big fan of using this API to manipulate docker, especially as an automation engineer. Despite it's many issues, it's pretty robust for an early version of the API.

The recent issue I found is any type of redirects in a command passed in to ```Client.execute()``` are ignored and mess up big time. Here's a really simple example (this is using iPython, btw, in case you were wondering the formatting):
```
In [5]: import docker

In [7]: cli = docker.Client("tcp://0.0.0.0:13873")

In [9]: cli.execute("cust1_L3", "ls -l")
Out[9]: 'total 21100\nlrwxrwxrwx   1 root root        7 Jan 29 08:33 bin -> usr/bin\n-rw-r--r--   1 root root 21535764 Mar 26 05:22 csd-L3.rpm\ndrwxr-xr-x   5 root root      520 Mar 25 14:17 dev\n-rw-r--r--   1 root root    14524 Nov 25 18:06 epel-release-7-5.noarch.rpm\ndrwxr-xr-x  67 root root     4096 Mar 25 14:17 etc\ndrwxr-xr-x   3 root root     4096 Mar 25 14:17 home\nlrwxrwxrwx   1 root root        7 Jan 29 08:33 lib -> usr/lib\nlrwxrwxrwx   1 root root        9 Jan 29 08:33 lib64 -> usr/lib64\ndrwx------   2 root root     4096 Jan 29 08:33 lost+found\ndrwxr-xr-x   2 root root     4096 Jun 10  2014 media\ndrwxr-xr-x   2 root root     4096 Jun 10  2014 mnt\ndrwxr-xr-x   3 root root     4096 Feb 21 18:02 opt\ndr-xr-xr-x 215 root root        0 Mar 25 14:17 proc\ndr-xr-x---   5 root root     4096 Feb 21 18:04 root\ndrwxrwxr-x  15 root root     4096 Mar 25 14:17 run\nlrwxrwxrwx   1 root root        8 Jan 29 08:33 sbin -> usr/sbin\ndrwxr-xr-x   3 root root     4096 Jan 29 08:36 selinux\ndrwxr-xr-x   2 root root     4096 Jun 10  2014 srv\ndr-xr-xr-x  11 root root        0 Mar 25 14:17 sys\ndrwxrwxrwt   9 root root     4096 Mar 26 16:46 tmp\ndrwxr-xr-x  14 root root     4096 Feb 21 18:03 usr\ndrwxr-xr-x  20 root root     4096 Mar 25 14:17 var\n'

In [10]: cli.execute("cust1_L3", "ls -l | grep sys")
Out[10]: 'ls: cannot access |: No such file or directory\nls: cannot access grep: No such file or directory\nsys:\ntotal 0\ndrwxr-xr-x   2 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 block\ndrwxr-xr-x  22 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 bus\ndrwxr-xr-x  43 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 class\ndrwxr-xr-x   4 root root 0 Mar 26 17:49 dev\ndrwxr-xr-x  12 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 devices\ndrwxr-xr-x   4 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 firmware\ndrwxr-xr-x   5 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 fs\ndrwxr-xr-x   7 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 kernel\ndrwxr-xr-x 135 root root 0 Mar 25 14:17 module\n'
```

Now I'm fairly certain this has to do with the shlex.split, as this usually gets turned into a list that gets interpreted as index 0 being the command and everything after being arguments, so the pipe and the grep are being treated as additional arguments to ls rather than a pipe.

I'm gonna start digging for a possible solution, but I think this is worth noting and fixing, as the cli ```docker exec``` can handle this problem:
```
[user@somemachinesomewhere ~]$ sudo docker exec -i cust1_L3 bash -c "ls -l"
total 21100
lrwxrwxrwx   1 root root        7 Jan 29 08:33 bin -> usr/bin
-rw-r--r--   1 root root 21535764 Mar 26 05:22 csd-L3.rpm
drwxr-xr-x   5 root root      520 Mar 25 14:17 dev
-rw-r--r--   1 root root    14524 Nov 25 18:06 epel-release-7-5.noarch.rpm
drwxr-xr-x  67 root root     4096 Mar 25 14:17 etc

drwxr-xr-x   2 root root     4096 Jun 10  2014 mnt
drwxr-xr-x   3 root root     4096 Feb 21 18:02 opt
dr-xr-xr-x 218 root root        0 Mar 25 14:17 proc

issue_title: exception thrown when pulling repo from registry.hub.docker.com
issue_body:
Example:  `client.pull('registry.hub.docker.com/my/repo')`
would raise docker.errors.InvalidRepository: Invalid repository name, try "my/repo" instead

The if body starting at line 62 in docker-py/docker/auth/auth.py is where this occurs.  It prohibits us from explicitly naming registry.hub.docker.com or index.docker.io as registries.  I assume this is because they are implied.

What is the rationale behind throwing an exception when the registry is stated explicitly?  The `docker pull` bash command allows us to explicitly name the registry even if it is implied.

    """
    Returns a class given an input fully-qualified class path E.g.
    a.b.c.d.ClassName

    :param fully_qualified_class_path:
    :return:
    """
    module_name, class_name = fully_qualified_class_path.rsplit('.', 1)
    imported_module = import_module(module_name)
    return getattr(imported_module, class_name)


def main(application_runner_class: type):
    """
    Given an input CrossBar ApplicationRunner class, runs a Component
    defined using input from either the command-line or environment variables
    with parameters determined by said input.

    :param application_runner_class:
    :return:
    """
    parser = generate_parser()
    args = parser.parse_args()
    component = get_class(args.component)
    extra = None
    if args.extra_file is not None:
        extra = json.load(open(args.extra_file))
    runner = application_runner_class(extra=extra, **{
        key: value
        for key, value in vars(args).items()
        if key not in ('component', 'extra_file', 'log_level')
    })
    txaio.start_logging(level=args.log_level)
    return runner.run(component)
```

`asyncio.py`
```python
# coding=utf-8
from __future__ import unicode_literals

from autobahn.asyncio.wamp import ApplicationRunner

from runners import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main(ApplicationRunner)
```

`twisted.py`
```python
# coding=utf-8
from __future__ import unicode_literals

from autobahn.twisted.wamp import ApplicationRunner

from ethel.runners import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main(ApplicationRunner)
```

issue_title: [Is this a bug?]: BiDAF appears to access wrong metadata instances when running on multiple GPUs
issue_body:
This loop https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/43fc89ee59944dca0de01ca5ab2843c9926323d6/allennlp/models/reading_comprehension/bidaf.py#L274 iterates over the range obtained from a tensor dimension corresponding to batch size https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/43fc89ee59944dca0de01ca5ab2843c9926323d6/allennlp/models/reading_comprehension/bidaf.py#L176 accessing elements of the `metadata` list with indices in this range. This is reasonable in a CPU or single GPU scenario where `forward()` operates on the entire batch generated by the trainer algorithm. With multiple GPUs, all tensors passed into `forward()` are sliced and scattered across GPU instances with `forward()` transparently receiving only a subset of data (e.g. with configured batch size of 20 on 8 GPUs, we see actual `batch_size` in `forward()` to be either 2 or 3). Metadata is copied into each call to `forward()` in its entirety since it does not contain tensors. Therefore the majority of the metadata access happens with a wrong index: the tensors operated on are not conformal to the array of metadata instances.

I wonder if the results of BiDAF training on multiple GPUs were previously found deficient or inferior to training them on a single GPU with otherwise identical configuration. This apparent disconnect in the code may explain such an outcome. I have only run across this issue by comparing BiDAF code with the multi-paragraph version in https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/pull/804 which actually generates a runtime exception because of it. Of course, being an absolute newbie in this codebase I may be completely wrong!

issue_title: User "census"?
issue_body:
For most if not all of the core contributors, trackpy can be considered part of our professional output, which means that measuring its impact helps justify our efforts. Especially for assistant professors. A [Google Scholar search](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?hl=en&q=%22trackpy%22&btnG=&as_sdt=1%2C5&as_sdtp=) turns up 11 results, which is pretty good! (The core contributors are authors on just 5 of those papers, in case you were curious.) However, I suspect there is more usage that we're not seeing — work where trackpy went uncited, work in preparation, uses outside of research, etc. Since trackpy is such a young project, it seems important to consider these users as well.

As we prepare to announce v0.3, would it be worthwhile to attempt a census of trackpy users? Something like

> This release represents a tremendous amount of effort by several developers over the past year (thanks!). For the core developers especially, trackpy is a professional activity, and we hope it can stay that way. So we need to keep track of the impact trackpy is having. Citations measure only part of this impact, so **if you consider yourself a trackpy user, please take 15–60 seconds to let us know!**

And there would be a link to a Google form (or whatever) asking for

- Affiliation
- Name
- How are you using trackpy?
- Do you have any publications or conference presentations that used trackpy?
- Do you have any general comments or feedback for the trackpy developers? (Please note: this is not the place to request a specific feature or report a bug. Instead, create an issue on github.)

The form can stress that **all fields are optional** except the first, and that we'll publicly use this information only in the aggregate (as in, "trackpy is used by at least N researchers at M different organizations").

What do you think? Too much? Is there a better channel? Or should we just keep pushing for more citations?

issue_title: Deis Router to route on paths.
issue_body:
Would be great to be able to route requests through nginx based on the path supplied in addition to the domain. The reasoning is that different paths could be rewritten to a container specifically for that domain/path combination. An example would be...

`http://www.example.com/` - goes to the regular container as expected for the `www` app.
`http://www.example.com/blog` - goes to a different container based on the domain and path combination.

Would like to get thoughts on this as a feature as it would allow a lot more flexibility in the way the Deis platform as a whole can be used.

So, right now `git push` will deploy a container for that sub-domain, and I don't see that needing to change. The idea is that you could `git push` a `blog` app which would live in Deis under `http://blog.example.com/`, but with an additional config setting we could route that path through to this container making it's content display at `http://www.example.com/blog`.

issue_title: unexpected upper() called in to_checksum_address(address)
issue_body:
https://github.com/ethereum/eth-utils/blob/c06d26119cd8062cf4e75d87a620c2d5892151ee/eth_utils/address.py#L118

norm_address[i] should not invoke `upper()`. 

In that case, address is '0x8eb009288b68c343de5bc393a7e13c28f5425780', checksum_address is '0x8EB009288b68C343DE5Bc393a7e13C28F5425780'. 

`is_checksum_address(value)` returns `False`. The two addresses should be compared case-insensitively, although unnecessary

issue_title: *MRS serializers inconsistently (dis)allow filenames in dump() function
issue_body:
Some serializers, such as `penman.dump()`, allow a filename as the first parameter instead of a file, but others (e.g., `simplemrs.dump()`, `mrx.dump()`) only allow file objects (despite docstring claims).

These should be made consistent.

The problem is fixed by removing the parens from non-callable objects in utils.py

```
def find_parent_shell(path=False, max_stack_depth=10):
    """return process name or path of parent.  Default is to return only name of process."""
    try:
        import psutil
    except ImportError:
        stderrlog.warn("No psutil available.\n"
                       "To proceed, please conda install psutil")
        return None
    process = psutil.Process()
    pname = process.parent.name.lower()
    stack_depth = 0 
    while (any(proc in pname for proc in ["conda", "python", "py.test"]) and 
           stack_depth < max_stack_depth):
        if process:
            process = process.parent()
            pname = process.parent.name.lower()
            stack_depth += 1
        else:
            # fallback defaults to system default
            if sys.platform == 'win32':
                return 'cmd.exe'
            else:
                return 'bash'
    if path:
        return process.parent.exe()
    return process.parent.name
```

issue_title: Conda install doesn't recognize dependency when installed with pip
issue_body:
In our CI environments I run into the issue that astropy 1.3 is not yet available with the latest and greatest numpy and python versions. Thus I fall back on installing it with pip.
However when other packages are also needed to be installed, conda install doesn't recognize that their astropy dependency is already available in the environment.

Is there a way to work around this issue? On example build is e.g. this:
https://travis-ci.org/bsipocz/ci-helpers/jobs/203928062

issue_title: Jupyter config doesn't play nicely with conda environments
issue_body:
I have installed a few of the Jupyter extensions from here into a Conda environment where I could try them out:

https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter/issues/124

This is a normal sort of thing to do: there is a lot of power in extensions.

The problem is that these create "shared" configuration information that causes problems when I then run Jupyter from inside a Conda environment where those extensions are **not** installed.  Below is some terminal output to demonstrate the issue.

Because Anaconda is the recommended way to get Jupyter, because we want people to use Jupyter with Anaconda, because we think extensions are the bees knees, and because Conda environments are awesome -- we need to figure out how to make Jupyter extensions play nicely with Conda environments which right now (for popular extensions) they do not.

@bollwyvl and @damianavila, this is definitely one for you to consider, and perhaps is tied to my woes here: https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/nb_conda/issues/23

issue_title: Local package cache higher in priority than conda-build folders
issue_body:
If I have two recipes, "upstream" and "downstream", I see very undesirable behavior with conda.

1. build upstream - hash abc123, timestamp 
2. build downstream
3. change upstream, rebuild - hash (somethingnew)
4. build downstream

The second "downstream" build will use the initial "upstream" package, not the more recent build.  I suspect that it is because conda is finding the older version first in the pkgs folder.  I have not been able to track down where this happens.  Here's where I've traced it:

1. get_index: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/core/index.py#L39
2. get_channel_priority_map prepends "local": https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/core/index.py#L126
3. 'local' multichannel defined using conda_build_local_urls: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/301133cb8c804730c218ad8e47f116a8b1af51fc/conda/base/context.py#L453
4. conda_build_local_paths: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/base/context.py#L197

None of these show pkgs.  Where is this coming in?

issue_title: Already locally satisfied dependencies must be available in remote repositories for "install"
issue_body:
I am installing a package which requires `flask-login` (any version).  It is already available locally in `conda-meta` and `pkgs` directories for the env I am installing to:

```
flask-login-0.2.9-py27_33.tar.bz2
flask-login-0.2.9-py27_33.json
```
However when I go install a package that lists `flask-login` as a dependency, I'm told:
```
Error: No packages found in current linux-64 channels matching: flask-login
Did you mean one of these?
    flask-ldap-login
```
It is surprising to me that conda doesn't realize that the package is already installed, or at least that it is already available in the local package cache.

If I provide a binstar channel where `flask-login` exists, `conda install` happily proceeds and at *that point* realizes that it is already installed so no additional action is required.

At the moment, updating Numpy can cause package dependencies to end up in an inconsistent state:

```
$ conda create -n testdeps2 python=2.7
Fetching package metadata: ....

Fetching package metadata: ....

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/tom/miniconda3/envs/testdeps2:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    astropy: 1.0.4-np19py27_0

Fetching package metadata: ....

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    numpy: 1.9.3-py27_0 --> 1.10.1-py27_0

Unlinking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%
Linking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%
mac-robitaille2:tmp tom$ conda list
# packages in environment at /Users/tom/miniconda3/envs/testdeps2:
#
astropy                   1.0.4                np19py27_0  
numpy                     1.10.1                   py27_0  
openssl                   1.0.2d                        0  
pip                       7.1.2                    py27_0  
python                    2.7.10                        2  
readline                  6.2                           2  
setuptools                18.4                     py27_0  
sqlite                    3.8.4.1                       1  
tk                        8.5.18                        0  
wheel                     0.26.0                   py27_1  
zlib                      1.2.8                         0  
```

The issue here is that Astropy is still the one built against Numpy 1.9, so when using some modules which were compiled against the Numpy C API, one gets warnings along the lines of:

```
module compiled against API version a but this version of numpy is 9
```

TL;DR: if updating Numpy, packages that depend on a specific version of Numpy should be updated too.

issue_title: conda doesn't use anaconda-client url
issue_body:
I started to get errors with my CI when it came to the conda build step.  After digging, with @msarahan help, discovered that the step that install conda-build was also updating conda to 4.2.6.  This version appears to have issues using the url set via anaconda-repo.  Was able to reproduce locally too.

```
anaconda config --set url <private anaconda repo>/api
anaconda login
conda install channelpackage -c mychannel
```

throws the following error:

```
○ → conda install channelpackage -c mychannel
WARNING conda.connection:get_binstar_token(106): Warning: could not capture token from anaconda-client (RecursionError('maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object',))
WARNING conda.connection:get_binstar_token(106): Warning: could not capture token from anaconda-client (RecursionError('maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object',))
CondaHTTPError: HTTP Error: Could not find URL: https://conda.anaconda.org/mychannel/linux-64/
```

issue_title: Guidance on porting code to new SDKs
issue_body:
Please could you point us at where to find guidance on porting code to newer SDK versions?

In https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/commit/4dbe837e5b256da2865a300ab68078b7a58c14ea#diff-ddfd642a99bc6edf8ec1a1b4ab58aba8 we see that `azure.mgmt.authorization.models.RoleAssignmentProperties` has gone away; however Google doesn't lead me to find anywhere Microsoft documentation about what it's replaced with. I assume `RoleAssignmentCreateParameters` but do not find any issue here or elsewhere about if/why this changed.

Our code was originally working with `azure-cli-2.0.6` but now I'm trying to make it work with `azure-cli-2.0.31`.

This migration is somewhat because 2.0.6 doesn't seem to work:

```
$ virtualenv azure-cli-2.0.6 && ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/pip install azure-cli==2.0.6 && ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/az
[...]
bash: ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/az: Permission denied
```

```
$ chmod +x ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/az && ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/az
/usr/bin/python: No module named azure
```

```
$ . ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/activate
(azure-cli-2.0.6)  $ ./azure-cli-2.0.6/bin/az
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 174, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", fname, loader, pkg_name)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/runpy.py", line 72, in _run_code
    exec code in run_globals
  File "/home/user/src/azure-cli-2.0.6/lib/python2.7/site-packages/azure/cli/__main__.py", line 9, in <module>
    import azure.cli.main
  File "/home/user/src/azure-cli-2.0.6/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/azure/cli/main.py", line 9, in <module>
    from azure.cli.core.application import APPLICATION, Configuration
ImportError: No module named application
```

Strangely though, if I ignore the non-working `az`, I do note that this 2.0.6 has `azure-mgmt-authorization              0.40.0` which lacks `RoleAssignmentProperties`, so I'm confused that it seems azure-cli==2.0.6 now gets different supporting libraries installed.

Overall, I think we're struggling to understand how to have our code stay working/stay up to date with changes in the SDK. What's the guidance for managing that? Thank you!

issue_title: Contributing: Add some information about running the tests to this repo
Compilation problem on macOS High Sierra with -march=core2
issue_body:
They haven't been able to build a bottle for bullet yet on macOS 10.13 High Sierra due to a [compilation problem with b3GjkPairDetector.cpp when compiling for a core2 cpu architecture](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/issues/18493#issuecomment-332764454). The problem manifests with clang hanging, unable to terminate normally. The compilation problem seems to trigger with `-O2` and higher, but is ok with `-O1` and `-O0`. On my machine, it works fine with `-march=native`, but homebrew bottles are built to be compatible with older architectures.

This might be a clang bug in XCode 9, but I figured I'd report it here first. Here's a few compiler commands for reproducing the problem, assuming you start from the root of the bullet source tree:

~~~
$ c++ --version
Apple LLVM version 9.0.0 (clang-900.0.37)
Target: x86_64-apple-darwin17.0.0
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin
~~~

## Compiler flags that work

### -O3 -DNDEBUG -march=native

~~~
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/c++ \
  -I./src \
  -o b3GjkPairDetector.o \
  -c ./src/Bullet3OpenCL/NarrowphaseCollision/b3GjkPairDetector.cpp \
  -O3 -DNDEBUG -march=native
~~~

### -O1 -DNDEBUG -march=core2

~~~
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/c++ \
  -I./src \
  -o b3GjkPairDetector.o \
  -c ./src/Bullet3OpenCL/NarrowphaseCollision/b3GjkPairDetector.cpp \
  -O1 -DNDEBUG -march=core2
~~~

### -O0 -DNDEBUG -march=core2

~~~
/Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/c++ \
  -I./src \
  -o b3GjkPairDetector.o \
  -c ./src/Bullet3OpenCL/NarrowphaseCollision/b3GjkPairDetector.cpp \
  -O0 -DNDEBUG -march=core2
~~~

## Compiler flags that don't work

extra_compile_args=["-O3"]
issue_body:
On Windows (conda/gcc), the default compilation switch is "-O", which yields a much slower extension module than what can be obtained after compiling with "-O3".  I would thus suggest adding `extra_compile_args=["-O3"]` to the `setup.py` so that this is used by default.
Not sure whether this would affect those using the MSVC compiler though.

issue_title: Sharing db connection to nested nested app
issue_body:
## Long story short

I've read  your answer in #2413 (raised in #2412 ).
The solution (`subapp['db'] = mainapp['db']`) does not work when it comes to nested subapps.
For example, if I have to build an app A, which have some subapp B and I need to pass the db connection from A to B, which can be done in that behavior. But, when I need to nest app A as a subapp to C, another app, and how should I pass db from C to A and to B. Clearly, the sharing between A and B exists before that between C and A, when A does not know the existence of db (which is even set inside startup) and C should also know nothing about the inner structure of A. A more complicated mechanism should be provided. 

## Expected behaviour

A more complicated solution. 

## Actual behaviour

Too simple to solve the problem. 

## Steps to reproduce

```python
from aiohttp import web

app_b = web.Application()

app_a = web.Application()
app_b['db'] = app_a['db']  # error
app_a.add_subapp('/b', app_b)

app_c = web.Application()
app_c['db'] = 'db'  # actually inside a startup event
app_c.add_subapp('/a', app_a)  # how should I pass app_c['db']

web.run_app(app_c)

```
## My solution
I'm trying to solve the problem by making the db become a `promise` (or a monad, which makes more sense about the side effect of the main app on the sub app ?). 
```python
from aiohttp import web


class MyApplication(web.Application):

    get_db = None

    def set_db(self, db):
        self.get_db = lambda: db

    def add_subapp(self, prefix, subapp):
        # The db should be a `promise` (like a monad).
        # When the `db` property is called,
        # the promise is then calculated.
        # So, when the db is set, a promise is set,
        # and when it is passed to the subapp, the promise is passed

        async def set_db(app):
            app.get_db = lambda: self.get_db()
        subapp.on_startup.append(set_db)
        #  subapp.get_db = lambda: self.get_db()
        return super().add_subapp(prefix, subapp)

```
While flask provides a `g` so I can share the db between different apps (with in the same request), will you consider adding a same mechanism (or a monadic implementation if your consider about the implicit `side effect` introduced by `g`), since not only the db is shared(more data should be shared?).

issue_title: Client deadlocks when having received long read() then EOF before new request
issue_body:
## Long story short

When :
- the program creates a request wich returns a big content ;
- the OS `read` syscall returns with long buffer (containing a response) ;
- the program fetches the response but not its content (`response.status == 200` but no `response.text()` or something else) ;
- the program creates a new request...

## Expected behaviour

The program gets its second response (and the first one is of course not discarded).

## Actual behaviour

The call to `session.get()` deadlocks until it timeouts. 

## Steps to reproduce

Run the following program. It creates a simplistic server that serves big enough content to trigger the bug. The server is not part of the bug.

It also contains the problematic client calls that lead to the timeout. I'm using aiohttp server and localhost because this combination enables the OS to push a big read. (as an aside, the real sent content is a rather big generated PNG image)

```python
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web


@asyncio.coroutine
def main_client(loop):
	session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
	a = yield from session.get("http://localhost:8742/plop")
	print("A:", a.status)

	# don't release 'a' for now

	b = yield from session.get("http://localhost:8742/bla") # deadlocks here
	# following code is never called
	print("B:", b.status)

	b.release()
	a.release()
	session.close()


@asyncio.coroutine
def handler(request):
	# see aiohttp.streams.DEFAULT_LIMIT for the "magic value" of 2 ** 16
	# this works
	#return aiohttp.web.Response(body="Z" * ((2 ** 16) * 2))

	# this does not work
	return aiohttp.web.Response(body="Z" * ((2 ** 16) * 2 + 1))

@asyncio.coroutine
def main_server(loop):
	global server
	server = aiohttp.web.Server(handler)
	yield from loop.create_server(server, "0.0.0.0", 8742)


# case preparation : craft a server with big writes
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main_server(loop))

# execution of the client
loop.run_until_complete(main_client(loop))

# garbage collecting
loop.run_until_complete(server.shutdown(10))
```

## Details

The problem is due to two events :
- data received fills the buffer up (`aiohttp.streams.FlowControlStreamReader.feed_data()` and its super call), triggering a pause on the protocol.
- the reach of the end of the reponse triggers a `StreamReader.feed_eof()` which causes the protocol to be returned to the pool of the session while response data is still in the associated `FlowControlStreamReader` buffer.

I think that the protocol shouldn't be returned to the pool before `release()` is *explicitly* called on the response, letting the program choose when the connection can be reused. I see the commit 321550e1b5b759b7e09a66cfe3ebc9b1e16c94e6 that tries to release the connection earlier, but reached EOF doesn't mean that the FlowControlStreamReader buffer is ready again to be used.

## Your environment

Python 3.4.2, aiohttp 2.1.0
Python 3.5.3, aiohttp 2.3.0a0

issue_title: Cannot access/delete cookies with the same name
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is similar to https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1125 except that this relates to the server rather than the client.

The server cannot send back two cookies for different subdomains that have the same name.

## Expected behaviour

If multiple cookies are defined with the same name but different paths/domains, both cookies should be sent on the response.

## Actual behaviour

The response only includes the last cookie set

## Steps to reproduce

```python
response = web.Response()
response.set_cookie('foo', 'cookie for the domain', domain='localhost');
response.set_cookie('foo', 'cookie for the subdomain', domain='foo.localhost');
```

## Your environment

- Python 3.6.5
- aiohttp 3.2.1

issue_title: aiohttp 2.x closing client request data stream
issue_body:
In previous versions of aiohttp, if you passed a stream as the `data` parameter to an aiohttp request, it would not close the stream after the request, now it is closing it because it's wrapping it in a `Payload`, and later the `write` method closes it.

I believe aiohttp should not be closing the stream sent to it. This new behavior caused this issue with aiobotocore: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/issues/221 resulting in retries failing.

issue_title: What's the recommended way of passing information to middlewares
issue_body:
Today we've come to a situation where we are not sure on how to solve it in a clean way using aiohttp, let me give some context:

For all endpoints we have, we want to send an event. This event is only sent depending on some values that come as an output from our view that uses `json_response`. We also want this event to contain some data generated in the view.

Since there is no field `extras` in the response object were we can store raw data so it can be used in the middlewares, we've thought of different approaches without any of them feeling "clean".

1. Send the event in each view explicitly (there is no middleware involved here) -> We haven't gone this way because it doesn't scale, imagine if we reach a point where we have lots of views and we change the data the event sends or how it is being sent.

2. Propagate the information through headers -> Super ugly, we are modifying our output to the clients just to be able to send an internal event.

3. Hack the response object once built to add a new field called `extras` or something like that so then we are able in the middleware to access `response.extras` and work from there.

Below is a pseudocode for our middleware:

```python
async def apicall_handler(request):

  response = await handler(request)

  if some_response_value is True
    send_metric_with_some_response_data()
    return response  

  return apicall_handler
```

Right now we are going for number 3 but it still feels hacky.

So my question is, is there a clean way of doing this? If not, would it make sense to add an `extras` (or whatever name you prefer) in the Response object so users can propagate information to the middlewares?

issue_title: Adding a "output_updated.OutputArea" event
issue_body:
The ``display`` function provides a very convenient way of updating output using the ``display_id`` and the resultant display handle. The updates take a very different codepath to regular ``display_data`` and ``execute_result`` messages however, and therefore do not provide a way to subscribe to a general event which makes the whole mime bundle and the OutputArea available.

In JupyterLab this is possible because the ``renderModel`` method of any registered custom mime renderers is given the whole mime bundle. It would be quite convenient to have an equivalent mechanism, which could easily be implemented by adding an ``output_updated``  event for ``OutputArea``. If this idea sounds reasonable I'd be happy to put together a PR.

issue_title: While list commands that requires skbuild to run CMake
issue_body:
Instead of black listing commands, as it is currently done in [skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/fe4b930ea5216fa47456846507851116d28c9895/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L310-L315), commands that requires cmake to be executed should be explicitly listed.

Considering the list of `bdist`, `build` and `install` commands:

```bash
$ python setup.py --help-commands | ack "^\s+(build|bdist|install)"
  build                     build everything needed to install
  build_py                  "build" pure Python modules (copy to build directory)
  build_ext                 build C/C++ extensions (compile/link to build directory)
  build_clib                build C/C++ libraries used by Python extensions
  build_scripts             "build" scripts (copy and fixup #! line)
  install                   install everything from build directory
  install_lib               install all Python modules (extensions and pure Python)
  install_headers           install C/C++ header files
  install_scripts           install scripts (Python or otherwise)
  install_data              install data files
  bdist                     create a built (binary) distribution
  bdist_dumb                create a "dumb" built distribution
  bdist_rpm                 create an RPM distribution
  bdist_wininst             create an executable installer for MS Windows
  bdist_wheel               create a wheel distribution
  install_egg_info          Install an .egg-info directory for the package
  bdist_egg                 create an "egg" distribution
  build_sphinx              Build Sphinx documentation
```

For the one requiring CMake to run, we can trim it to this list for the:

```
  build                     build everything needed to install
  install                   install everything from build directory
  install_lib               install all Python modules (extensions and pure Python)
  bdist                     create a built (binary) distribution
  bdist_dumb                create a "dumb" built distribution
  bdist_rpm                 create an RPM distribution
  bdist_wininst             create an executable installer for MS Windows
  bdist_wheel               create a wheel distribution
  bdist_egg                 create an "egg" distribution
```

For the remaining ones, we will simply delegate to the "regular" setup function ...
```
  build_py                  "build" pure Python modules (copy to build directory)
  build_ext                 build C/C++ extensions (compile/link to build directory)
  build_clib                build C/C++ libraries used by Python extensions
  build_scripts             "build" scripts (copy and fixup #! line)
  install_headers           install C/C++ header files
  install_scripts           install scripts (Python or otherwise)
  install_data              install data files
  install_egg_info          Install an .egg-info directory for the package
  build_sphinx              Build Sphinx documentation
```

def foo():

issue_title: [Bug report] iter_neighborhood() gives incorrect neighborhoods for both von Neumann and Moore
issue_body:
Moore's neighborhood is defined as:
![Moore neighborhood](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/images/equations/MooreNeighborhood/NumberedEquation1.gif)
Or see [this link](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/MooreNeighborhood.html) for a more complete explanation.

In the code however, it is calculated by excluding everything that is outside the Euclidean distance:
```Py
if moore and (dy ** 2 + dx ** 2) ** .5 > radius > 1:
    continue
```
This can be corrected by just removing the condition, as the correct indices are already visited.


Von Neumann's (extended) neighborhood is defined as:
![von Neumann neighborhood](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/images/equations/vonNeumannNeighborhood/NumberedEquation1.gif)
Or see [this link](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/vonNeumannNeighborhood.html) for a more complete explanation.

In the code however, it is calculated by excluding any diagonals, which makes it more like a huge cross:
```Py
if not moore and dy != 0 and dx != 0:
    continue
```
This can be corrected as follows:
```Py
if not moore and abs(dx) + abs(dy) > radius:
    continue
```

issue_title: Remove implprefix arg to PluginManager
issue_body:
As per discussion in #144 we plan to outright remove the `implprefix` kwarg to `PluginManager` after an appropriate PR has been made to `pytest` implementing the prefix based hook detection/registration.

Further discussion should be made here regarding a possible [strategy pattern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strategy_pattern) for hook detection outside of the regular explicit marking mechanism.

An initial thought is to instead offer prefix based registration through a `prefix` kwarg to `PluginManager.register()` if this is a feature we'd like to keep.

issue_title: Forms need to be loaded dynamically by ALL views modules
issue_body:
### Issue Summary

Dashboard flatpages forms are not loaded dynamically into the respective view module, but directly imported. This is also true for the reviews forms.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Override an existing form in your own `dashboard.pages.forms`
2. Navigate through your dashboard to the view which should show said form
3. Cry?

I'm not really sure why all the other dashboard apps dynamically load forms using `get_class`, and yet `pages` and `reviews` import them directly. This makes it difficult to override those forms, since we now require a view override as well as a form override.

These two view modules _really_ should import their respective forms using `get_class`, unless there's an extremely good reason to favor direct import.

### Technical details

* Python version: 3.6.4
* Django version: 1.11.13
* Oscar version: 1.5.4

issue_title: Advanced content filter issue
Building on windows is hard
issue_body:
Building and deploying on windows systems is still not easy, since there is often no compiler available and, currently, only gcc is supported by `setup.py`. Switching from a cython to a [ctypes](https://docs.python.org/3.3/library/ctypes.html) binding could remedy this issue. Another possibility might be [CFFI](https://cffi.readthedocs.org/en/).

Implications of using a ctypes binding:
- tedious python code
- might need to write a separate MAD-X extension to expose a more convenient C API (or integrate into MAD-X upstream, but they are unlikely to accept the contribution)
- might be possible without improved C API, with even more tedious python code 
- no more static binding
- decreased performance (not significantly, I guess)

issue_title: Domain name validation in Email validator
issue_body:
Hello developers,

This is more of a question (possibly an issue). I was trying to validate some email addresses using the `Email` validator class, when I stumbled on it not validating this particular email address: `test@domain.c`

I then looked into [the code](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/validate.py#L119) and found that the part after the `.` in the domain name must be at least 2 characters.

May I ask what the reasoning behind this is?

I know email validation (and by extension domain name validation) can be a complicated issue, so I tried looking up if there are formal specifications that can be relied upon. I found that the format of the domain name is specified in [this RFC, section 2.1](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1123#page-13) and by extension [this RFC, section B](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc952.txt). There seems to be no restrictions there on limiting the part after the `.` to 2 characters.

I am on Python 3.5.2, running marshmallow version 2.10.3. Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Duration handling for broken flac files
issue_body:
Hi,

I stumble upon some broken flac files in the wild that doesn't report a correct length (duration).

Prior to 1.36 they were reporting a duration of 0.0:
```
>>> import mutagen
>>> audio = mutagen.File("test.flac")
>>> audio.info.length
0.0
```

With 1.36 it's actually raising a ZeroDivisionError:

```
>>> import mutagen
>>> audio = mutagen.File("test.flac")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/_util.py", line 131, in wrapper_func
    return func(h, *args, **kwargs)
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/_file.py", line 295, in File
    return Kind(fileobj, filename=filething.filename)
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/_file.py", line 48, in __init__
    self.load(*args, **kwargs)
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/_util.py", line 152, in wrapper
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/_util.py", line 123, in wrapper
    return func(self, h, *args, **kwargs)
  File "venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mutagen/flac.py", line 801, in load
    float(fileobj.tell() - start) * 8 / self.info.length)
ZeroDivisionError: float division by zero
```

I think this was introduced by adding the bitrate info in this commit: https://github.com/quodlibet/mutagen/commit/d8aed5afcf32cdddea30fdaf812e1f2c25c6d3e6

If you decide to handle this type of broken files should the bitrate be 0 ?

issue_title: Incremental QF_BV Soundess Bug
issue_body:
Setting the logic to QF_BV can produce incorrect results for incremental solving.

For example, with the following queries I expect ```unsat unsat```, but get ```unsat sat``` instead:

```
(set-logic QF_BV)

(declare-fun A () (_ BitVec 3))
(declare-fun B () (_ BitVec 3))
(declare-fun C () (_ BitVec 3))

(assert (and
		(= (bvadd A #b001) B)
		(= A #b000)
	))

(push 1)
(assert (not (= A #b000)))
(check-sat)
(pop 1)

(assert (= ((_ extract 2 2) C) #b1))
(assert (= B C))

(check-sat)
```

Changing the logic to ```BV```, or not setting the logic at all gives the correct result.
Without the first incremental assertion and ```check-sat``` call, it also produces the correct result.

It appears to be similar to [#1368](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1368) but for ```QF_BV``` instead of  ```LIA```.

issue_title: Unnecessary dependency on py.test
issue_body:
The API has a hard dependency on `py.test` -- consumers of the library will thus download `py.test` (and `py`, as a transitive dependency) whenever they `pip install` it.

Unless the library does actually need `py.test` to regularly function (which I doubt 😸) the dependency should moved away from those parsed by `setup.py`.

issue_title: Feature proposal: Differentiable AUC approximation loss function for binary classification
issue_body:
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) metric is non-differentiable, but a differentiable approximation exists (Calders & Jaroszewicz, 2007) that is based on the AUC's interpretation as the probability that the classifier ranks a randomly chosen true positive higher than a randomly chosen false positive (H.L. van Trees, 1968).

I have implemented the function on the bottom of the second page of Calders & Jaroszewicz (2007) using Theano functions, and have verified that the output values match those of sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score for large enough sample sizes.  Assuming I correctly follow the contribution guidelines, would a pull request with this feature be accepted?

issue_body:
Currently in nteract code that causes kernel failure will leave the notebook with a quietly broken kernel.

To  reproduce, execute: 
```
import sys
from ctypes import CDLL

libc.time(-1)  # BOOM!!
```

issue_title: Distinguishing `CRAWLERA_DOWNLOAD_TIMEOUT` and `X-Crawlera-Timeout`
issue_body:
Currently we have:
1. `CRAWLERA_DOWNLOAD_TIMEOUT` to specify the download timeout between a client and Crawlera nodes.
2. [`X-Crawlera-Timeout`](http://doc.scrapinghub.com/crawlera.html#x-crawlera-timeout) to specify the download timeout between Crawlera nodes and the target websites (not yet supported by this package).

Looks like they are somehow misleading and confusing. As we may not easily change the names, shall we:
1. Add some kind of notes to explain the difference.
2. Also support `X-Crawlera-Timeout` in this package.

issue_title: TypeError: conv() got an unexpected keyword argument 'num-timesteps'
issue_body:
Just a little problem with the README in the saving loading visualizing section : it says to run 
``` 
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --num-timesteps=2e7 --save_path=~/models/pong_20M_ppo2
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --num-timesteps=0 --load_path=~/models/pong_20M_ppo2 --play
```
But it should say:
```
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --num_timesteps=2e7 --save_path=~/models/pong_20M_ppo2
python -m baselines.run --alg=ppo2 --env=PongNoFrameskip-v4 --num_timesteps=0 --load_path=~/models/pong_20M_ppo2 --play
```
With an underscore in num_timesteps.
Otherwise it will result in the aforementioned error (title).
Took me 5 minutes to figure out what was wrong hope it will save somebody those 5 minutes !

Cheers!

issue_title: How to compose multiple queries together in v1.0
issue_body:
I'm getting this error message when I try to query my schema on http://localhost:8000/graphiql

    "message": "Query fields must be a mapping (dict / OrderedDict) with field names as keys 
     or a function which returns such a mapping."


I've upgraded to 1.0 using https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/UPGRADE-v1.0.md

this is what my schema looks like

    from graphene import relay, ObjectType
    from graphene_django.filter import DjangoFilterConnectionField

    from master_database.schemas.buildings import BuildingsNode

    class MasterDatabaseQuery(ObjectType):
       building = relay.Node.Field(BuildingsNode)
       all_buildings = DjangoFilterConnectionField(BuildingsNode)



and this is what my BuildingsNode looks like after the upgrade



    import graphene
    from graphene.relay import Node
    from graphene_django import DjangoObjectType

    from master_database.models import Buildings


   
    class BuildingsNode(DjangoObjectType):
      uuid = graphene.String(description='uuid')

      def resolve_uuid(self, args, info):
          return self.instance.id

      class Meta:
          interfaces = (Node, )

issue_title: Status update on Incremental and Grid Search
issue_body:
Quick status update: I wanted to explore a workflow that would use scikit-learn
as much as possible. We'd use scikit-learn for all the hyper-parameter
optimization and the actual training. Dask would just provide the large
arrays.

The end goal is something as close as possible to `GridSearchCV(SGDClassifier())` trained on a larger-than-memory dataset.

```python
X, y = load_dask_arrays()

clf = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

First, we have to avoid passing a large Dask array to `SGDClassifier.fit`, as it
would be converted to an ndarray. So we wrap it with `Incremental`, which passes
blocks to the `SGDClassifier.partial_fit`:

```python
import dask_ml.wrappers

X, y = load_dask_arrays()

clf = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
inc = dsak_ml.wrappers.Incremental(clf)
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

At this point, `inc.fit(X, y)` works fine (I'm currently debugging unexpectedly
high memory usage, but let's ignore that for now), but there's a subtle issue with
`inc.score(X, y)`, which surfaces in `GridSeachCV`. By default, a pass-through
scorer is used, which is `SGDClassifier.score`. This ends up using
`sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score`, which end up converting the test Dask arrays
into large ndarrays on a single worker. This is captured in #200.

A workaround to the scoring issue is to manually pass a scorer that is able to
work well with Dask arrays. We've implemented a few in Dask-ML:

```python
import dask_ml.wrappers
import dask_ml.metrics
from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer

X, y = load_dask_arrays()
scorer = make_scorer(dask_ml.metrics.accuracy_score)

clf  = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
inc = dask_ml.wrappers.Incremental(clf, scoring=scorer)
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

This gets us serial hyper-parameter optimization on larger-than-memory Dask
arrays. To do things in parallel, we can use the distributed backend.


```python
import dask_ml.joblib
from sklearn.externals import joblib


with joblib.parallel_backend("dask"):
    gs.fit(X, y)
```

I'll post benchmarks later when I've run them.

issue_title: Check unchecked types in certbot-dns-cloudflare
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 263357278, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20testing', 'name': 'area: testing', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-cloudflare` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_cloudflare.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Implement account deactivation
issue_body:
Boulder recently merged code to allow for [ACME draft-03 style](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-03#section-6.2.2) account deactivation.

The process is extremely simple, to deactivate their account a user must POST a signed payload containing `{"status":"deactivated"}` to the `/acme/reg/{id}` endpoint. The server will respond with 200 and the contents of the deactivated registration if successful.

issue_title: Allow third parties to update the counterparty transfer
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

To support offline nodes without closing the netting channels a monitoring third party is required, the third party will be responsible to call `updateTransfer` (or a equivalent function) on behalf of the offline counter party.

## Solution

Allow nodes that are not participants to call a function that has a similar behavior to `updateTransfer`.

## Tasklist

- [ ] A new message format for a node to inform third parties about it's known latest balance proofs.
- [ ] A new netting channel function for a third party to update the transfer on behalf of the participant.
  - [ ] Forbid the third-party from overwriting a participant provided transfer. (Note: This is not to protect against bad behaving participants, but to protect against bad behaving third-parties)

Allow third parties to close the channel #182

issue_title: Synchronize API calls that use non-constant smart contract functions
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current API functions that call non-constant smart contract functions need synchronization, this is required to avoid sending transactions for the same effect.

A user interacting with the REST API might make two or more HTTP requests for opening channels with the same token and partner, since only the first transaction can succeed the additional ones will only burn gas.

## Solution

Add synchronization for the following functions:

- get_new_events
- open
- deposit
- close
- settle
- transfer / transfer_and_wait / transfer_async

## TODO

semantics for the concurrency control

-  linearize the calls:
    - synchronized for the same cursor
        - get_new_events
- prohibit concurrent calls
    - channel manager with the same partner address
        - open
    - on the channel
        - deposit, close, and settle

issue_title: Check if the channel_identifier that requested it is old before making an on-chain transaction
issue_body:
This is not in a direct connection with [raiden contracts 193](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/issues/193) but stemmed out of the conversation from it as the easiest solution for #1907.

Essentially everytime we are about to make an on-chain call, check if the channel we are about to make the call for is the current channel and if not, do not perform the transaction.

For example in the case of #1907 it was due to checking the events since last restart and seeing there is a need to call `updateNonClosingBalanceProof()` since the counterparty closed while we were offline. But in the meantime the counterparty had reopened the channel and as such the `updateNonClosingBalanceProof()` is for a previous channel and will always fail.

To avoid that we need to add a check before [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/proxies/token_network.py#L586) for the `updateNonClosingBalanceProof()` case and make a call to the `TokenNetwork` proxy for the current channel identifier. If it's the same as the one that lead us to go down the path of making the on-chain transaction we will proceed. If not, we will just do nothing. Perhaps add a debug log saying the channel is outdated so we do nothing.

Do the same as above for all the blockchain transactions in [token_network.py](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/proxies/token_network.py)

issue_title: Execute tests on top of a tester network
issue_body: End-to-end tests currently run on top of a geth/hydrachain private network, while this is a good solution it has some speed and introspection problems. To facilitate debugging of contracts and speed up the write-test-repeat cycle we should exploit the `ethereum.tester` directly, writing a new `BlockchainService` facade for the `tester`.

## Problem Definition

The button for leave token network does not work. I receive the following response:
